task_name: Clean facial wrinkles and skin folds
description: Use a soft, damp cloth or pet-safe wipe to clean between facial folds, nose rope, and any other skin folds. Dry thoroughly afterwards to prevent moisture buildup.
Observe your Frenchie's breathing at rest and after mild exercise. Note any noisy breathing, snoring severity, exercise tolerance changes. Rate on a simple 0-3 scale.
Frenchies have narrow ear canals that trap moisture. Check for wax buildup, debris, or odor. Use a vet-approved ear cleaner and cotton ball — never Q-tips.
Weigh your Frenchie on a home scale. Obesity exacerbates BOAS, IVDD, and joint problems. Maintain visible waist and palpable ribs.
Inspect eyes for redness, discharge, cloudiness, or cherry eye (red mass in corner). Frenchies' prominent eyes are injury-prone.
Trim nails, check paw pads for cracks or interdigital cysts. Frenchies can get pododermatitis from allergies.
For Frenchies with tight screw tails — clean under the tail fold where debris and moisture collect. Use pet-safe wipes and dry thoroughly.
Lift lips to check gums and teeth. Brachycephalic breeds have crowded teeth predisposing to periodontal disease.
Gently palpate along the spine. Note any pain response, abnormal curvature, or muscle atrophy. IVDD and hemivertebrae can develop silently.
Monitor for new itching, licking, ear infections, or skin changes. Frenchies are allergy-prone. Track potential triggers (food, environment, season).
Goldens have a dense double coat that sheds year-round with heavy seasonal sheds. Daily brushing removes loose fur, prevents matting, and distributes natural oils.
Goldens' floppy ears trap moisture, making them prone to otitis. Check for redness, odor, or discharge. Clean with vet-approved solution weekly — especially after swimming.
Weigh weekly. Goldens love food and gain weight easily. Obesity accelerates hip/elbow dysplasia. Keep at ideal body condition score (4-5/9).
Monitor for stiffness, difficulty rising, reluctance to climb stairs, or bunny-hopping gait. Early hip/elbow dysplasia intervention preserves joint health.
Palpate lymph nodes under jaw, in front of shoulders, in armpits, and behind knees. Enlarged nodes are often the first sign of lymphoma. 1 in 8 Goldens develop lymphoma.
Check for cloudiness, redness, discharge, or vision issues. Cataracts, PRA, and distichiasis are all Golden concerns.
Brush teeth 3-4x weekly. Goldens can develop periodontal disease. Check for tartar, red gums, broken teeth.
Long nails alter gait and put stress on joints. Trim monthly. If you hear clicking on floors, nails are too long.
Feed 2-3 smaller meals instead of 1 large. No vigorous exercise 1 hour before and after meals. Use slow-feed bowl if dog gulps food. Know GDV warning signs.
Goldens are prone to atopy. Watch for new itching, hot spots, ear infections, or paw licking. Note seasonal patterns.
Count breaths per minute while Cavalier is sleeping. Normal: <30 breaths/min. Elevated RRR is the earliest sign of MVD-related heart failure. Track in app daily.
Note any coughing, especially at night or when excited. MVD causes fluid in lungs → cough. Track frequency and triggers.
Gently pet your Cavalier's neck and head. Watch for flinching, air scratching, or vocalizing. Syringomyelia causes neuropathic pain that owners often miss.
Silky coat tangles easily behind ears, legs, and chest. Daily brushing prevents mats.
Long, floppy ears restrict airflow. Check for wax, debris, redness, or odor. Cavaliers are prone to ear infections secondary to allergies.
Check for cloudiness (cataracts), redness, discharge, or vision changes. Annual OFA eye certification recommended.
Obesity worsens MVD, SM, and joint problems. Keep Cavaliers lean (body condition 4-5/9). This is especially important with heart disease.
Small dogs are prone to dental disease. Brush teeth daily if possible. Dental bacteria can worsen MVD (endocarditis risk).
Count breaths per minute while sleeping. Normal: <30/min for cats. Elevated RRR is an early sign of HCM-related heart failure. Track daily.
Ragdolls have a semi-long, silky coat that mats easily. Daily brushing prevents tangles and reduces hairballs. Pay special attention to armpits and belly.
Ragdolls are large cats prone to obesity. Excess weight strains the heart (compounding HCM risk). Maintain lean body condition.
HCM can cause a heart murmur or gallop rhythm. Vet checks for murmurs. A murmur in a Ragdoll = echocardiogram until proven otherwise.
Watch for hind limb weakness, dragging paws, or sudden paralysis. Saddle thrombus (aortic thromboembolism) is a catastrophic HCM complication — cat loses use of hind legs suddenly.
Monitor urine output, straining, or blood in urine. FLUTD and urinary issues are stress-related in Ragdolls. Changes in litter box habits are often the first sign of illness.
Check gums and teeth. Dental disease is common and bacteria can affect the heart.
Count breaths/min while sleeping. Normal <30. HCM is the #1 cause of death in Maine Coons. Elevated RRR is an early sign of developing heart failure.
Watch for gait changes: bunny-hopping, stiffness, reluctance to jump. Hip dysplasia affects ~18% of Maine Coons. SMA causes progressive hind weakness in affected kittens.
Thick, water-resistant double coat needs daily brushing to prevent matting and reduce hairballs. Maine Coons shed heavily.
Maine Coons are the largest domestic cat breed — males can reach 11+ kg. Obesity stresses hips and heart. Keep lean — you should feel ribs easily.
Large cats are prone to dental issues. Check gums, teeth, and breath. Periodontal disease bacteria can affect heart and kidneys.
Monitor litter box habits. Male Maine Coons are at elevated FLUTD/blockage risk. Any straining = emergency vet visit (blocked cat dies in 24-48 hours untreated).
HCM can cause heart murmur or gallop rhythm. A new murmur in a Maine Coon warrants echocardiogram. Annual echo recommended for breeding cats from age 1.
Trim claws. Large cats need well-maintained claws for proper gait. Overgrown claws can snag, tear, or cause joint stress.
Large breed cats benefit from joint supplements (glucosamine, omega-3s). Review supplement plan monthly — adjust based on mobility and age.
